content:
# Numbers Chapter 22 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 174–176 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_022_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_022_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_022_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0129–0130 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_022_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_022_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## Balak Summons Balaam (vv. 1–14)

**22:1** And the Sons of Israel set out and encamped in the plains of Moab, beyond the Jordan at Jericho.

**22:2** And Balak the son of Zippor saw all that Israel had done to the Amorites.

**22:3** And Moab was exceedingly afraid because of the people, for they were many. And Moab was sick with dread because of the Sons of Israel.

**22:4** And Moab said to the elders of Midian, "Now this assembly will lick up all that is around us, as the ox licks up the green of the field." And Balak the son of Zippor was king of Moab at that time.

**22:5** And he sent messengers to Balaam the son of Beor, to Pethor, which is by the River, to the land of the sons of his people, to call him, saying, "Behold, a people has come out from Egypt. Behold, they cover the eye of the land, and they are dwelling opposite me.

**22:6** And now, come, please, curse this people for me, for they are mightier than I. Perhaps I shall be able to strike them, and I shall drive them out from the land. For I know that he whom you bless is blessed, and he whom you curse is cursed."

**22:7** And the elders of Moab and the elders of Midian went with the fees for divination in their hand. And they came to Balaam and spoke to him the words of Balak.

**22:8** And he said to them, "Lodge here tonight, and I will bring back word to you, as YHWH shall speak to me." And the princes of Moab stayed with Balaam.

**22:9** And God came to Balaam and said, "Who are these men with you?"

**22:10** And Balaam said to God, "Balak the son of Zippor, king of Moab, has sent to me, saying,

**22:11** 'Behold, the people that has come out from Egypt -- it covers the eye of the land. Now come, curse them for me. Perhaps I shall be able to fight against them and drive them out.'"

**22:12** And God said to Balaam, "You shall not go with them. You shall not curse the people, for they are blessed."

**22:13** And Balaam rose in the morning and said to the princes of Balak, "Go to your land, for YHWH has refused to let me go with you."

**22:14** And the princes of Moab rose and went to Balak and said, "Balaam refused to come with us."

---

## The Second Embassy (vv. 15–21)

**22:15** And Balak again sent princes, more numerous and more honored than these.

**22:16** And they came to Balaam and said to him, "Thus says Balak the son of Zippor: 'Let nothing, I pray you, hinder you from coming to me,

**22:17** for honoring I will surely honor you greatly, and all that you say to me I will do. And come, please, curse this people for me.'"

**22:18** And Balaam answered and said to the servants of Balak, "If Balak were to give me his house full of silver and gold, I could not go beyond the mouth of YHWH my God, to do a small thing or a great thing.

**22:19** And now, stay here, you also, tonight, that I may know what more YHWH will speak with me."

**22:20** And God came to Balaam at night and said to him, "If the men have come to call you, rise, go with them. But only the word that I speak to you, that you shall do."

**22:21** And Balaam rose in the morning and saddled his donkey and went with the princes of Moab.

---

## The Donkey and the Angel (vv. 22–35)

**22:22** And the anger of God burned because he was going, and the angel of YHWH stationed himself in the road as an adversary against him. And he was riding upon his donkey, and his two servants were with him.

**22:23** And the donkey saw the angel of YHWH standing in the road, with his drawn sword in his hand. And the donkey turned aside out of the road and went into the field. And Balaam struck the donkey to turn her back onto the road.

**22:24** And the angel of YHWH stood in a narrow path of the vineyards, a wall on this side and a wall on that side.

**22:25** And the donkey saw the angel of YHWH, and she pressed herself against the wall and pressed the foot of Balaam against the wall. And he struck her again.

**22:26** And the angel of YHWH passed on further and stood in a narrow place where there was no way to turn either to the right or to the left.

**22:27** And the donkey saw the angel of YHWH, and she lay down under Balaam. And the anger of Balaam burned, and he struck the donkey with the staff.

**22:28** And YHWH opened the mouth of the donkey, and she said to Balaam, "What have I done to you, that you have struck me these three times?"

**22:29** And Balaam said to the donkey, "Because you have made a fool of me. If only there were a sword in my hand, for now I would have killed you."

**22:30** And the donkey said to Balaam, "Am I not your donkey, upon which you have ridden all your life to this day? Have I ever been in the habit of doing thus to you?" And he said, "No."

**22:31** And YHWH uncovered the eyes of Balaam, and he saw the angel of YHWH standing in the road, with his drawn sword in his hand. And he bowed his head and fell on his face.

**22:32** And the angel of YHWH said to him, "Why have you struck your donkey these three times? Behold, I went out as an adversary, for your way is reckless before me.

**22:33** And the donkey saw me and turned aside before me these three times. If she had not turned aside from before me, surely now I would have killed you and let her live."

**22:34** And Balaam said to the angel of YHWH, "I have sinned, for I did not know that you were standing in the road to oppose me. And now, if it is evil in your eyes, I will return."

**22:35** And the angel of YHWH said to Balaam, "Go with the men. But only the word that I speak to you, that you shall speak." And Balaam went with the princes of Balak.

---

## Balak Receives Balaam (vv. 36–41)

**22:36** And Balak heard that Balaam had come, and he went out to meet him at the city of Moab, which is on the border of the Arnon, which is at the extremity of the border.

**22:37** And Balak said to Balaam, "Did I not surely send to you to call you? Why did you not come to me? Am I not truly able to honor you?"

**22:38** And Balaam said to Balak, "Behold, I have come to you. But am I at all able to speak anything? The word that God puts in my mouth, that I shall speak."

**22:39** And Balaam went with Balak, and they came to Kiriath-Huzoth.

**22:40** And Balak sacrificed cattle and sheep, and sent portions to Balaam and to the princes who were with him.

**22:41** And it came to pass in the morning that Balak took Balaam and brought him up to Bamoth-Baal, and he saw from there the edge of the people.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Overall Assessment
All three translators -- EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA -- worked faithfully from the Leningrad Codex (pages 174–176) and cross-referenced Codex Vaticanus (pa_0129–0130). The manuscript images confirm the standard Masoretic text with no unusual scribal features. Page 174 of the Leningrad Codex shows the beginning of Numbers 22 in the three-column format characteristic of Samuel ben Jacob's hand, with Masoretic marginal notations (Masorah parva) visible throughout. Page 175 (photographed inverted as a verso) continues the narrative through the donkey episode, and page 176 carries the text through the end of the chapter and into chapter 23. The Vaticanus pages (pa_0129–0130) show the corresponding Greek text in continuous scriptio continua, with the chapter heading MZ visible at the start of the Balaam narrative.

The three translations are in substantial agreement throughout this chapter. EMET provided heavily annotated interlinear renderings preserving every Hebrew morpheme; LOGOS produced clean formal-equivalent prose; PNEUMA rendered in accessible contemporary English. Divergences are largely matters of register and style rather than textual reading. The consensus follows LOGOS most closely as the base text, incorporating EMET's precision on key theological and technical terms and PNEUMA's clarity where it aids comprehension without sacrificing fidelity.

### Key Decisions

1. **Verse 3 -- "sick with dread" vs. "filled with dread" vs. "was in dread":** The Hebrew va-yaqats (from quts) carries a visceral, physical quality of revulsion beyond simple fear. PNEUMA's "sick with dread" captures this well; LOGOS's "filled with dread" is adequate but less precise; EMET's "was-in-dread" is accurate but mechanical. The consensus adopts "sick with dread" (PNEUMA) as the best representation of the Hebrew nuance. See Disagreement Log.

2. **Verse 4 -- "assembly" vs. "horde" for ha-qahal:** EMET and LOGOS render the Hebrew qahal as "assembly"; PNEUMA renders "horde." The consensus retains "assembly" as the more precise translation of qahal, which is a neutral term for a gathered body. See Disagreement Log.

3. **Verse 5 -- "the land of the sons of his people" (erets benei ammo):** All three translators follow the Leningrad Codex reading ammo ("his people") rather than the variant ammon ("Ammon"). The consensus preserves this reading. The Vaticanus LXX also supports this reading.

4. **Verse 7 -- "fees for divination" vs. "divinations in their hand":** EMET preserves the literal Hebrew ("divinations in their hand"), while LOGOS and PNEUMA interpret metonymically as "fees for divination." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA, as the metonymic reading is well supported by ancient interpretive tradition and the LXX's manteia.

5. **Verse 8 -- "princes" (sarim):** The narrator shifts from "elders" (zeqenim) in v. 7 to "princes" (sarim) in v. 8. All three translators note this shift. PNEUMA renders "officials" throughout. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS in using "princes" for sarim, preserving the Hebrew distinction. See Disagreement Log.

6. **Verse 17 -- Infinitive absolute kabbed akhabedkha:** EMET renders "honoring I-shall-surely-honor-you"; LOGOS renders "I will surely honor you"; PNEUMA renders "I will surely, surely honor you." Per the binding convention requiring infinitive absolute doubling, the consensus adopts "honoring I will surely honor you" to preserve the Hebrew construction visibly. See Disagreement Log.

7. **Verse 18 -- "house" vs. "palace" for bayit:** EMET and LOGOS render "house"; PNEUMA renders "palace." The Hebrew bayit can mean both, but the consensus retains "house" as the more literal rendering, allowing the context (a king's house full of silver and gold) to convey the royal implication. See Disagreement Log.

8. **Verse 22 -- "burned" vs. "blazed" vs. "was kindled" for va-yikhar af:** EMET uses "burned"; LOGOS uses "was kindled"; PNEUMA uses "blazed." The consensus adopts "burned" as the most direct rendering of the Hebrew idiom kharah af. See Disagreement Log.

9. **Verse 25 -- "pressed ... crushed" vs. "pressed ... pressed":** PNEUMA renders the second verb as "crushing Balaam's foot," while EMET and LOGOS both use "pressed." The Hebrew uses the same root (lakhats) in both instances. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS. See Disagreement Log.

10. **Verse 29 -- "made a fool of me" vs. "dealt wantonly" vs. "mocked me":** The Hebrew hit'allalt carries senses of abuse, mockery, and humiliation. EMET's "dealt wantonly" is archaic; LOGOS's "mocked" is adequate; PNEUMA's "made a fool of me" captures both the humiliation and the contextual irony. The consensus adopts PNEUMA's rendering. See Disagreement Log.

11. **Verse 30 -- Infinitive absolute ha-hasken hiskanti:** EMET renders with full doubling ("have I been accustomed, have I been accustomed"); LOGOS renders "Have I been accustomed to doing thus to you?"; PNEUMA renders "Have I ever been in the habit of doing this to you?" Per binding conventions, the consensus preserves the doubling structure: "Have I ever been in the habit of doing thus to you?" -- retaining the sense of the infinitive absolute emphasis through "ever" while avoiding awkward mechanical doubling of this particular verb. See Disagreement Log.

12. **Verse 32 -- "Why" vs. "Upon what" for al-mah:** EMET renders "Upon-what"; LOGOS and PNEUMA render "Why." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA as "Why" is the natural English equivalent of this Hebrew interrogative idiom.

13. **Verse 37 -- Infinitive absolute shaloakh shalakhti:** EMET renders "sending I-sent"; LOGOS renders "Did I not surely send"; PNEUMA renders "Did I not send urgently." The consensus adopts "Did I not surely send" (LOGOS), which preserves the emphatic force of the infinitive absolute per binding conventions. See Disagreement Log.

14. **Verse 38 -- Infinitive absolute yakol ukhal:** EMET renders "being-able am-I-able"; LOGOS renders "Am I at all able"; PNEUMA renders "do I have any power at all." The consensus adopts "Am I at all able" (LOGOS) as preserving the emphatic construction while remaining idiomatic. See Disagreement Log.

15. **Divine names pattern:** The consensus preserves the Leningrad Codex's careful alternation between YHWH and Elohim throughout. God (Elohim) is used when the narrator describes God approaching Balaam (vv. 9, 10, 12, 20, 22). YHWH is used in the angel episodes (vv. 22–35) and in Balaam's own speech (vv. 8, 13, 18, 19). The Vaticanus LXX renders YHWH as Kyrios and Elohim as ho theos, with occasional interchange (notably v. 28, where Vaticanus reads ho theos where the MT has YHWH). The consensus follows the MT.

---

## Disagreement Log

### Verse 3 -- "sick with dread" vs. "filled with dread" vs. "was in dread"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and-was-in-dread Moab on-account of the-Sons of Israel" | Morphological gloss of va-yaqats |
| LOGOS | "And Moab was filled with dread because of the Sons of Israel" | Formal equivalent; interprets quts as "filled with dread" |
| PNEUMA | "Moab was sick with dread because of the Sons of Israel" | Dynamic rendering; captures visceral quality of quts |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted PNEUMA's "sick with dread." The Hebrew verb quts carries a connotation of physical revulsion and nausea-like anguish that goes beyond ordinary fear. The LXX's prosochthisen ("was vexed/disgusted") confirms the visceral register. "Sick with dread" preserves this emotional intensity better than "filled with dread."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of PNEUMA.

---

### Verse 4 -- "assembly" vs. "horde" for ha-qahal

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-assembly will-lick-up all our-surroundings" | Literal rendering of ha-qahal |
| LOGOS | "this assembly will lick up all that is around us" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "this horde will devour everything around us" | Dynamic; "horde" conveys threat; "devour" interprets lakhakh |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "assembly" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew qahal is a neutral collective noun; "horde" introduces pejorative coloring not present in the source. However, the consensus retains "lick up" rather than PNEUMA's "devour," as the Hebrew lakhakh specifically means "to lick" and the simile with the ox licking grass depends on this precise image.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

### Verse 5 -- "Behold" vs. "Look" for hinneh

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold" | Standard rendering of hinneh |
| LOGOS | "Behold" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Look" | Dynamic contemporary equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Behold" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew hinneh is a presentative particle that carries a rhetorical weight beyond simple "look"; "Behold" preserves this register in English and is used consistently throughout the SOPHIA Bible.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Applies throughout the chapter (vv. 5, 6, 11, 32, 38).

---

### Verse 6 -- "surely blessed / surely cursed" vs. "blessed / cursed" vs. "truly blessed / truly cursed"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "whom you bless is blessed, and whom you curse is cursed" | Preserves passive participles without intensifier |
| LOGOS | "he whom you bless is surely blessed, and he whom you curse is surely cursed" | Adds "surely" to represent the established certainty |
| PNEUMA | "whoever you bless is truly blessed, and whoever you curse is truly cursed" | "truly" for emphasis; "whoever" for universality |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET's rendering without the intensifier. The Hebrew uses simple passive participles (mevorakh, yu'ar) without an adverbial intensifier. The certainty is expressed by the participial construction itself, not by an added word. Adding "surely" or "truly" is interpretive, not translational.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET.

---

### Verse 7 -- "fees for divination" vs. "divinations in their hand"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and-divinations in-their-hand" | Literal rendering of u-qesamim be-yadam |
| LOGOS | "with the fees for divination in their hand" | Metonymic interpretation |
| PNEUMA | "with fees for divination in their hands" | Same metonymic interpretation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA's "fees for divination." The metonymic use is well-attested in ancient interpretation and the LXX's manteia supports the meaning of professional divination payment. The literal "divinations in their hand" is misleading in English, suggesting they carried oracles rather than payment.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

### Verse 8 -- "princes" vs. "officials" for sarim

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-princes of Moab" | Standard rendering of sarim |
| LOGOS | "the princes of Moab" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "the officials of Moab" | Modern equivalent; avoids monarchical connotation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "princes" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew sarim denotes high-ranking royal courtiers sent on behalf of the king; "princes" captures this status and distinguishes them from the "elders" (zeqenim) of v. 7. "Officials" is too generic and loses the social register.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Applies throughout the chapter (vv. 8, 13, 14, 15, 21, 35, 40).

---

### Verse 16 -- "Let nothing hinder you" vs. "Do not be withheld" vs. "Let nothing stop you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do-not please be-withheld from-going unto-me" | Literal rendering of al na timana |
| LOGOS | "Let nothing, I pray you, hinder you from coming to me" | Formal equivalent with smooth English |
| PNEUMA | "Please -- let nothing stop you from coming to me" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Let nothing hinder you" captures the passive sense of the Hebrew niphal (timana, "be restrained/withheld") while reading naturally in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### Verse 17 -- Infinitive absolute kabbed akhabedkha (honoring/honor)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "honoring I-shall-surely-honor-you exceedingly" | Full infinitive absolute doubling |
| LOGOS | "I will surely honor you greatly" | Emphatic "surely" but no visible doubling |
| PNEUMA | "I will surely, surely honor you richly" | Double "surely" to represent infinitive absolute |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a modified rendering: "honoring I will surely honor you greatly." Per binding conventions requiring infinitive absolute doubling to be preserved, the construction must be visible. EMET's form is closest to the Hebrew structure; the consensus smooths the English slightly while keeping the characteristic doubling pattern.

**Status:** Resolved with modified EMET rendering per binding conventions.

---

### Verse 18 -- "house" vs. "palace" for bayit

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his-house full of silver and-gold" | Literal rendering of bayit |
| LOGOS | "his house full of silver and gold" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "his palace full of silver and gold" | Interpretive; bayit of a king = palace |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "house" (EMET/LOGOS). While "palace" is a contextually plausible interpretation, bayit is the general Hebrew word for "house." The royal context already implies the scale. Translating as "palace" introduces an interpretive step not warranted by the Hebrew lexeme.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

### Verse 22 -- "burned" vs. "was kindled" vs. "blazed" for va-yikhar af

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And-burned the-anger of God" | Literal rendering of va-yikhar af |
| LOGOS | "And the anger of God was kindled" | Traditional English Bible phrasing |
| PNEUMA | "God's anger blazed" | Dynamic; intensified register |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "burned" (EMET). The Hebrew va-yikhar is a qal verb meaning "to burn/be hot." "Burned" is the most direct rendering and avoids both the slightly archaic "was kindled" and the interpretive intensification of "blazed." The same idiom recurs in v. 27 for Balaam's anger, and consistency favors a single rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET.

---

### Verse 25 -- "pressed ... pressed" vs. "pressed ... crushing"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "she-pressed unto the-wall and-pressed the-foot of Balaam" | Same root (lakhats) rendered consistently |
| LOGOS | "she pressed herself against the wall and pressed the foot of Balaam" | Consistent rendering |
| PNEUMA | "pressed herself against the wall, crushing Balaam's foot" | Dynamic; "crushing" intensifies |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's consistent "pressed." The Hebrew uses the same root lakhats (niphal then qal) in both clauses. Using two different English words obscures this repetition, which is stylistically deliberate in the Hebrew.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

### Verse 29 -- "dealt wantonly" vs. "mocked" vs. "made a fool of me" for hit'allalt

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you-have-dealt-wantonly against-me" | Archaic but precise; hitpael of alal |
| LOGOS | "you have mocked me" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "you have made a fool of me" | Dynamic; captures humiliation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted PNEUMA's "made a fool of me." The Hebrew hit'allalt (hitpael of alal) conveys deliberate, abusive mockery -- making someone look foolish. "Mocked" is adequate but slightly too mild; "dealt wantonly" is archaic. "Made a fool of me" captures the personal humiliation Balaam feels, and the irony is sharper: the professional seer claims his donkey has made a fool of him, not knowing how truly foolish he appears.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of PNEUMA.

---

### Verse 30 -- Infinitive absolute ha-hasken hiskanti

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Have-I-been-in-the-habit of-doing thus to-you?" (with note on infinitive absolute doubling) | Full morphological rendering |
| LOGOS | "Have I been accustomed to doing thus to you?" | Formal equivalent; no visible doubling |
| PNEUMA | "Have I ever been in the habit of doing this to you?" | Dynamic; "ever" adds emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a rendering that preserves the infinitive absolute emphasis: "Have I ever been in the habit of doing thus to you?" The Hebrew ha-hasken hiskanti is an infinitive absolute construction, but mechanical doubling ("being in the habit, have I been in the habit") is unworkable in English. The word "ever" captures the emphatic force of the doubling, and "thus" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over "this" (PNEUMA) as it better represents the Hebrew koh.

**Status:** Resolved with modified rendering incorporating elements from all three.

---

### Verse 32 -- "Why" vs. "Upon what" for al-mah; "reckless" vs. "precipitous" for yarat

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Upon-what have-you-struck ... the-road is-precipitous before-me" | Literal al-mah; "precipitous" for yarat |
| LOGOS | "Why have you struck ... your way is reckless before me" | Idiomatic "Why"; "reckless" for yarat |
| PNEUMA | "Why have you struck ... your journey is reckless before me" | Dynamic; "journey" for derekh |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Why" is the natural English equivalent of al-mah. For the hapax yarat, all three translators agree on a sense of reckless/precipitous action; "reckless" is the clearest English rendering. "Way" (LOGOS) is preferred over "journey" (PNEUMA) or "road" (EMET) as it preserves the dual sense of derekh (both physical road and moral course).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### Verse 34 -- "to oppose me" vs. "to meet me" vs. "to confront me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "standing to-meet-me in-the-road" | Literal liqrati ("to meet me") |
| LOGOS | "standing in the way to oppose me" | Interpretive; supplies adversarial sense |
| PNEUMA | "standing in the road to confront me" | Dynamic; "confront" is stronger |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering: "standing in the road to oppose me." The Hebrew liqrati literally means "to meet me," but in context (the angel is a satan, an adversary) the meeting is oppositional. "Oppose" captures this contextual force without over-interpreting. "Confront" (PNEUMA) is slightly too aggressive for the Hebrew.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### Verse 37 -- Infinitive absolute shaloakh shalakhti

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "sending I-sent unto-you" | Mechanical doubling of infinitive absolute |
| LOGOS | "Did I not surely send to you" | Emphatic "surely" |
| PNEUMA | "Did I not send urgently to you" | Dynamic; "urgently" interprets emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering: "Did I not surely send to you." Per binding conventions, the infinitive absolute emphasis must be preserved. "Surely" captures the emphatic force. PNEUMA's "urgently" adds a nuance (urgency) not necessarily present in the construction, which emphasizes certainty rather than manner.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### Verse 38 -- Infinitive absolute yakol ukhal

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "being-able am-I-able to-speak anything?" | Mechanical doubling |
| LOGOS | "Am I at all able to speak anything?" | "at all" captures emphatic force |
| PNEUMA | "do I have any power at all to say anything?" | Dynamic restructuring |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering: "Am I at all able to speak anything?" This preserves the emphatic force of the infinitive absolute construction while remaining idiomatic. PNEUMA's "have any power" strays from the Hebrew ukhal ("be able"); EMET's mechanical doubling is unworkable in fluent English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### Verse 40 -- "cattle and sheep" vs. "cattle and flock"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "cattle and-flock" | Literal baqar va-tson; tson = "flock" (collective) |
| LOGOS | "cattle and sheep" | Standard English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "cattle and sheep" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "cattle and sheep" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). While EMET's "flock" is technically more precise (tson is a collective noun covering sheep and goats), "sheep" is the standard English rendering in sacrificial contexts and avoids the awkward pairing of a singular count noun ("cattle") with a collective ("flock").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

### Verse 41 -- "the edge of the people" vs. "the end of the people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-end of the-people" | Literal qetseh ha-am |
| LOGOS | "the edge of the people" | "edge" conveys spatial extremity |
| PNEUMA | "the edge of the people" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "edge" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew qetseh means "end/extremity," but in a spatial-visual context (seeing from a high vantage point), "edge" communicates more clearly that Balaam saw only the outer fringe of the encampment, not its full extent.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.
